edges.description: Loss of PFKM function blocks the phosphofructokinase step in skeletal-muscle glycolysis.
PFKM deficiency reduces erythrocyte glycolytic phosphofructokinase function.
Impaired skeletal-muscle glycolysis limits exertional energy production.
Exertional glycolytic energy failure contributes to muscle cramping.
Inability to use glucose as a muscle energy source is associated with exertional myalgia.
Exertional muscle injury can lead to myoglobinuria.
Severe exertional muscle injury can manifest as rhabdomyolysis.
The PFKM block may reroute glucose-6-phosphate through the pentose phosphate pathway during exertion, with downstream nucleotide and amino-acid metabolism contributing to abnormal ammonia generation.
intermediate_mechanisms: pentose phosphate pathway entry downstream of the PFKM block
nucleotide and amino-acid metabolism with ammonia generation
edges.target: Pentose phosphate rerouting and exercise nitrogen catabolism

edges.description: The PFKM step block produces an atypical lactate response during and after exercise.
edges.target: Low exercise lactate with delayed post-exercise rise

edges.description: Decreased glycogen metabolism during exercise is associated with high ammonia levels.
intermediate_mechanisms: Overuse of protein and purine nucleotide/amino-acid pathways during impaired glycogen metabolism.

edges.description: Abnormal exercise metabolism can be accompanied by hyperuricemia in the clinical Tarui disease syndrome.
Impaired glycolytic utilization leaves excess glycogen in skeletal muscle.
Skeletal-muscle glycolytic failure manifests as muscle weakness.
Muscle energy dysfunction is associated with myotonia in Orphanet's GSD VII phenotype record.
Chronic skeletal-muscle involvement can include muscle atrophy.
PPP entry distal to PFKM provides a proposed route to delayed post-exercise lactate generation while the primary PFKM block keeps lactate low during exercise.
intermediate_mechanisms: glucose-6-phosphate flux through the pentose phosphate pathway
edges.source: Pentose phosphate rerouting and exercise nitrogen catabolism
edges.target: Low exercise lactate with delayed post-exercise rise

edges.description: Increased nucleotide and amino-acid turnover downstream of the rerouted exercise metabolism generates ammonia.
edges.source: Pentose phosphate rerouting and exercise nitrogen catabolism

edges.description: Erythrocyte glycolytic enzyme deficiency manifests clinically as compensated hemolysis or hemolytic anemia.

edges.target: Pentose phosphate rerouting and exercise nitrogen catabolism

edges.description: Low exercise lactate with a delayed post-exercise rise reports impaired skeletal-muscle glycolytic flux at the PFKM step.
edges.target: Low exercise lactate with delayed post-exercise rise

edges.description: High ammonia during and after exercise tracks with the abnormal exercise metabolism caused by the PFKM glycolytic block.
Increased muscle glycogen content reports failed glycogen utilization caused by the skeletal-muscle PFKM block.
Elevated uric acid corresponds to the hyperuricemia reported with Tarui disease muscle glycolytic dysfunction.

evidence.reference_title: Tarui disease and distal glycogenoses: clinical and genetic update.
evidence.snippet: So far, more than one hundred patients have been described with prominent clinical symptoms characterized by muscle cramps, exercise intolerance, rhabdomyolysis and myoglobinuria, often associated with haemolytic anaemia and hyperuricaemia.

readouts.interpretation: Elevated uric acid corresponds to the hyperuricemia reported with Tarui disease muscle glycolytic dysfunction.

nodes.description: PFKM-related phosphofructokinase deficiency has an erythrocyte clinical arm: affected patients can have compensated hemolysis or hemolytic anemia in addition to skeletal-muscle exercise intolerance.

evidence.reference_title: Altered allosteric regulation of muscle 6-phosphofructokinase causes Tarui disease.
evidence.snippet: Tarui disease is a glycogen storage disease (GSD VII) and characterized by exercise intolerance with muscle weakness and cramping, mild myopathy, myoglobinuria and compensated hemolysis. It is caused by mutations in the muscle 6-phosphofructokinase (Pfk).

evidence.reference_title: Unique Exercise Lactate Profile in Muscle Phosphofructokinase Deficiency (Tarui Disease); Difference Compared with McArdle Disease.
evidence.snippet: In conclusion, we show that Tarui disease is associated with low lactate levels during exercise with a late increase of lactate after exercise and exceptionally high ammonia levels during and after exercise.

readouts.interpretation: High ammonia during and after exercise tracks with the abnormal exercise metabolism caused by the PFKM glycolytic block.

nodes.description: Ketogenic diet or modified Atkins dietary intervention may improve muscle symptoms and exercise tolerance in selected patients, under medical and nutritional supervision.

evidence.reference_title: Beneficial Effects of Ketogenic Diet on Phosphofructokinase Deficiency (Glycogen Storage Disease Type VII).
evidence.snippet: During the 5 years on KD, the patient's muscle symptoms had alleviated and exercise tolerance had improved.

evidence.reference_title: Beneficial Effects of Ketogenic Diet on Phosphofructokinase Deficiency (Glycogen Storage Disease Type VII).
evidence.snippet: Based on these findings, KD can be recommended under medical and nutritional supervision for selected patients with GSD VII, although further research of this rare disease is warranted.

evidence.reference_title: Beneficial Effects of Ketogenic Diet on Phosphofructokinase Deficiency (Glycogen Storage Disease Type VII).
evidence.snippet: Background: A deficiency of muscle phosphofructokinase (PFKM) causes a rare metabolic muscle disease, the Tarui disease (Glycogen storage disease type VII, GSD VII) characterized by exercise intolerance with myalgia due to an inability to use glucose as an energy resource.

nodes.description: Myoglobinuria is reported with exertional muscle injury in Tarui disease.

evidence.reference_title: Altered allosteric regulation of muscle 6-phosphofructokinase causes Tarui disease.
evidence.snippet: It is caused by mutations in the muscle 6-phosphofructokinase (Pfk). Pfk is an oligomeric, allosteric enzyme which catalyzes one of the rate-limiting steps of the glycolysis: the phosphorylation of fructose 6-phosphate at position 1.

evidence.reference_title: Case report: Comprehensive exploration of a novel PFKM mutation in glycogen storage disease Type VII.
evidence.snippet: Glycogen Storage Disease Type VII (GSD VII) is a rare glycogen metabolism disorder resulting from mutations in the PFKM gene, inherited in an autosomal recessive manner.

nodes.description: During exertion, the muscle PFKM block can leave fructose-6-phosphate and glucose-6-phosphate upstream of the block, with suggested rerouting through the pentose phosphate pathway and increased ammonia generation through protein, nucleotide, AMP, and amino-acid metabolism.
nodes.id: Pentose phosphate rerouting and exercise nitrogen catabolism

evidence.reference_title: Unique Exercise Lactate Profile in Muscle Phosphofructokinase Deficiency (Tarui Disease); Difference Compared with McArdle Disease.
evidence.snippet: The dotted arrow: fructose 6-phosphate accumulates because of Tarui disease, and its metabolism may continue in the PPP pathway, from where it may enter in protein or nucleic acid synthesis. Increase of synthesis of proteins or nucleotides means also increase of products of their metabolism, increasing, e.g., the production of ammonia.

evidence.reference_title: Unique Exercise Lactate Profile in Muscle Phosphofructokinase Deficiency (Tarui Disease); Difference Compared with McArdle Disease.
evidence.snippet: We suggest that also the activation of PPP route and the raise in ribose 5-phosphate could boost the ammonia levels by influencing the amino-acid synthesis. The deamination of AMP, as well as of various amino acids and the metabolites of citric acid cycle generate ammonia (13, 24).

nodes.description: Rhabdomyolysis is reported as an exertional complication.

evidence.snippet: So far, more than one hundred patients have been described with prominent clinical symptoms characterized by muscle cramps, exercise intolerance, rhabdomyolysis and myoglobinuria, often associated with haemolytic anaemia and hyperuricaemia.

nodes.description: Reduced PFKM activity interrupts skeletal-muscle glycolysis at the fructose-6-phosphate to fructose-1,6-bisphosphate step, limiting anaerobic use of muscle glycogen during exertion.

evidence.reference_title: Unique Exercise Lactate Profile in Muscle Phosphofructokinase Deficiency (Tarui Disease); Difference Compared with McArdle Disease.
evidence.snippet: In Tarui disease, reduced enzyme activity of muscle phosphofructokinase (PFKM) is detected resulting in impaired phosphorylation of fructose 6-phosphate to fructose 1,6-bisphosphate.

evidence.reference_title: Unique Exercise Lactate Profile in Muscle Phosphofructokinase Deficiency (Tarui Disease); Difference Compared with McArdle Disease.
evidence.snippet: Both patients with Tarui disease showed in muscle electron microscopic analysis extra lysosomal glycogen accumulations.
